title: Inside / Out at The Woods Cyclery in the New Forest – John Watson
content:
John and Cari had the honor and privilege of being taken under the wings of the wonderful people at The Woods Cyclery for the week leading up to Brother in the Wild earlier this month. This experience transcended the normal “third place” relationship bike shops can have within their communities. It left a lasting impression…

I can’t write about The Woods Cyclery without being emotional. Even now, in this story’s third iteration, tears are welling in my eyes. In some ways, visiting this shop was exactly what I needed. In other ways, it was emblematic of the direct impact our team’s dedication to radical atavism has had on the global cycling community. This passion has driven all of us to share spaces and the people who make them so special within this tiny, but growing niche of the bike industry at large. The Woods Cyclery felt like home even though it was thousands of miles away.

Thomas Ferrell’s passion and vision for The Woods Cyclery is truly something magical to behold, but like all good things, it took a lot of work to build it to what it is today.

New Forest

Growing up in a small home adjacent to an open, forested area, Tom has spent his life exploring the woods, recalling how he would spend his days exploring his big backyard. “Sometimes, as a kid, I’d just walk into the woods and try to get lost. My mom would let me camp out in the forest just behind our house, too.” Later in life, he grew up riding BMX and learning how to properly wield a bike. Watching Tom ride now, you can see the bike as a symbiotic extension of himself.

Later, Tom went to Morocco each winter to run a surf camp for a number of years, but he always returned to Southern England in the summer months. After sustaining a double whammy, wrecking his ACL twice in a year, he connected with the physical therapy of riding a fixed gear. These rides were spiritually therapeutic for Tom. Through fixed gears, he tapped into the world of alternative cycling and found websites like The Radavist that solidified his love for non-competitive cycling and the spaces they can help facilitate.

Tom responded to a calling he’d felt at the time. A desire to stay more rooted in the area surrounding the New Forest. He managed a bike hire shop for a while before opening one of his own just outside of Bournemouth. After a few years spent surfing winter swells along the many breaks of the coast while working at the bike hire shed, he sold it and, along with a business partner, opened The Woods Cyclery. Tom later took over The Woods as the sole owner.

When I asked what inspired him to open The Woods, Tom replied, “The Singular Peregrine is what did it all for me. Drop bars, big tires – I just loved the way it looked. Coming from BMX and riding track bikes, the Singular was it. Then, I found The Radavist and guys like Ronnie Romance, and it all clicked,” he said with a laugh, reflecting on a new world of cycling opening up to him, “seeing Ronnie riding a drop bar Krampus… that image just did it for me.”

“I wanted to be a part of what that guy was selling,” he said.

The Woods Cyclery

I’m sure many of you have a fondness for following bike shops around the globe on social media. Blue Lug in Tokyo, Circles/Sim Works in Nagoya, The Cub House in Los Angeles, and The Woods Cyclery in the UK are some of my favorites. Their social media accounts offer a curated, yet honest look into the day-to-day lives of those who make the shops true gems in the world of IBDs.

“When you start working at the shop, Tom opens The Radavist and says, ‘This is the Bible, study it,'” one of the mechanics, Owen, said to me as we were walking back from documenting his bike early one morning.

While Tom and the Woods staff look to The Radavist for inspiration, the admiration is felt reciprocally. I’ve been following The Woods and Tom on Instagram for several years. The featured builds from The Woods embody and represent a vibe that I’m wholly into. The Woods’s build on display at Bespoked UK this year was stunning! Off-road, big tires, metal chassis.  These bikes are simple solutions to so many complex problems the bike industry has inflicted on itself with overly stiff bikes and integrated fuckery.

Yet, the industry at large doesn’t really know what to do with The Woods Cyclery. They don’t sell carbon bikes or really promote racing. They like off-road riding but aren’t the downhill crowd. “Our reps are like, ‘There’s no point in showing you this, you won’t stock it,'” Tom said with a chuckle.

Occupying the alternative cycling space can be tricky for the broader bike industry to figure out. Maybe that’s not a bad thing, though.

UK Manufacturers

Meanwhile, during the pandemic, The Woods was Hope Tech’s third-largest retailer in the UK. “The Hope guys started coming around after COVID, and it took some time, but they began to figure it (The Woods) out.” Many of the shop’s builds feature UK brands like Hope and Middleburn. “We sell a ton of silver Hope parts.” If you recall from my Hope Tech shop visit last year, the brand doesn’t keep anodized components in stock; they anodize each part as orders come in. So a silver Hope order is easy for the manufacturer; they just grab the polished parts off the assembly line. This means many orders can go out the same day.

“We get Hope parts super fast, which makes the custom building process easy.”

My mind was spinning. The amount of on-hand bling The Woods displayed was wild! Feeling slightly overwhelmed, I needed a coffee and only had to walk a few steps to get one.

Food and (Damn Fine) Coffee

At this point in our trip, Cari and I had been galavanting around the UK and France for a few weeks. Every day, we’d try a new coffee shop in hopes of finding the perfect light-roast pour-over. We’d had some successes and many failures in Paris and Nice, but needless to say, I was elated to see a great selection of beans on display at The Woods Cafe.

On my first morning at the shop, Jude, one of the baristas, made me a pour-over that was the best coffee I’d had since visiting Prufrock in London. It was tangy, fruity, and aromatic. Once the coffee cooled a bit, the roast unfurled new flavors on the front of my tongue. I was in love. Then I had a toastie that was a flavor bomb. Coffee is only one part of the cafe’s prowess. The food, a mix of pasties and toasties, knocked our socks off as well.

We just didn’t expect this inside a bike shop.

“How’d this start?” I asked Tom about incorporating a cafe into his shop, who replied, “It all began on our group rides. We’d have these big carafes of coffee for the riders, and eventually, we saw the demand, so we built out the cafe.” Sacrificing valuable square footage for a cafe forces the bike shop to become more efficient with its space. Yet what this means for The Woods Cyclery is that the cafe attracts more than just cyclists.

Tradies, trekkers, stock traders, and more move through the shop, and with them come conversations that can be eavesdropped on about their workdays or agendas. Being in a travel destination allows for a steady stream of foot traffic but also presents some difficulties.

Build It

Owen, one of The Woods’ mechanics, replied when I asked how he ended up working at the shop, “People tend to sacrifice a bit for [Tom], and now we all know what it means to be here.” He continued, explaining how Lyndhurst is “…a tourist town, and our customers are made up of regulars and people who drove six hours, and you’ve just got to treat everyone like it’s their first time.”

“Wow, six hours?” I replied, surveying UK geography in my head. It’d taken us only an hour and a half to get to the shop from Heathrow in a rental car – it would have been an all-day endeavor using public transit. “Yeah, Tom has the ability to inspire people,” Owen finished. And inspire he does, attracting people from all over the UK.

Tom continued, “Almost every convo I’m like, ‘Where are you from?’ It’s always somewhere different.”

One of the sacrifices Owen alluded to is the cost of living in Lyndhurst. Most of the homes in Lyndhurst are expensive, too expensive to rent as a bike mechanic, and so most of the staff live a 20- or 30-minute drive from the shop. Although when they can afford the time to ride, it’s very pleasant, cutting through the 140,000 acres of the New Forest.

The shop itself is on a busy street – a fact you wouldn’t pick up on from the shop’s Instagram – but it can take several minutes to get a break in traffic to cross the road on foot. And if you needed any economic indicator of the Lyndhurst demographic, there’s a Ferrari dealership across the street with three big buildings. Our hotel, just up the street from The Woods, was full of retirees dressed in classic British garb, their fancy sports cars parked next to our hybrid Toyota rental car.

But all this makes sense for a shop to align itself in areas where the UK’s outdoor recreation economy is thriving, and the New Forest is one of the UK’s premier locations for hiking, cycling, birding, and more. People travel from all over to spend the weekend riding its cycling trail network, and The Woods is a stop on their itinerary – or it ought to be one.

A Busy Queue

“Wow, there are only two work stands?” I exclaimed after Jack, one of the shop’s mechanics, talked about the build queue during the busy months. “Sometimes we’re a few weeks out in the queue for custom builds, while working on day-to-day repairs. It can get crowded in here. And busy,” he said with a smile.

The shop runs very lean on space and requires efficiencies. Hannah came on board in July 2025 to help Robbie run the show. Robbie’s been with The Woods for five years. Together, they coordinate the staff while maintaining inventory and planning events. Cari and I were there during a busy time leading up to Brother in the Wild, and with Robbie getting married the following week, Hannah was in full-on production and planning mode.

Immediately, I felt a rapport with Hannah. They livened up any space they occupied and were always multitasking while herding the cats, so to speak. Their smile is infectious, and their hair really reflects their personality; lovely, bright, shimmering. The entire time I was poking around the shop documenting it, Hannah’s laugh could be heard in the background. They bring quite the vibe to the shop.

While Tom is the owner of the shop, he’s done a great job of empowering people who work for him. Everyone on staff complimented Tom’s ability to lead, and Tom handed the success of the shop to the staff.

There’s a real symbiosis present.

Community and Group Ride Culture

One of Tom’s more prideful components of The Woods is its Sunday group ride. People come in from all over the UK to go out for a pedal with the shop. The New Forest has over 100 miles of gravel cycling paths and over 140 miles of hiking-only trails. Needless to say, by connecting various residential roadways, bridleways, and New Forest cycling trails, The Woods offers a broad selection of routes for its group rides. The pace is slow and focused on building a community around the shop.

But anyone who throws a group ride knows community doesn’t just appear overnight, like a flower budding after many spring rains. “These things take years to build,”  Tom exclaimed. “So many factors go into it: timing, location, and people.” Making it all accessible and easy is just one part of the hurdle; New Forest is a busy place in the spring and summer months, and The Woods is at the gateway to this expansive land, so traveling with a bike is a hurdle not everyone can afford.

Even if you show up without a bike, The Woods Cyclery can help you out.

Rentals and Outfitting

Drawing on what he’d learned from owning a prior 250-bike rental fleet in Bournemouth, Tom has ensured The Woods has a full rental fleet of both pedal-assist hybrids and Surlys available. There’s even a full Wizard Works bag setup in the rental fleet, complete with racks for you to rent for a weekend of bike camping. You could literally show up, rent a bike, and be on your way in hours.

If you need to know where to go, Tom and the staff spend many nights out under the stars, and they have a nice guide of routes available at the check-out counter. There’s always a full-time mechanic working in the rental area, tuning up bikes and coordinating their rentals.

Or if you want to outfit your own personal bike for camping, The Woods stocks plenty of racks, bags, and other necessities. They can help get you set up.

The Employees

I’d spent two days shooting at The Woods Cyclery and trying to wrap my head around how this busy shop is able to achieve so much. Its digital footprint is formidable, with the shop’s Instagram showcasing countless stunning builds on display alongside a mix of scenery from the New Forest and all the lovely people the shop attracts. If you haven’t seen their account, click over after you finish reading the article – you’ll find yourself scrolling endlessly!

Each  employee’s bike represents their personalities. When I asked Tom what he wanted me to focus on in my story, he replied simply, “The employees. They’re the most important part of the shop.” Over the next few days, we’ll be looking at The Woods employees as well as their bikes, so stay tuned!

After Work Ride

While shop vignettes are an important element in our Inside/Out series at The Radavist, it’s the “out” that I have the most fun documenting. Even with the busy Brother in the Wild week, Tom rallied the troops to take me on a typical shop ride that proved to be a quintessential New Forest experience. We rode around 10 miles, and I was able to get a few photos I’m really proud of.

As the last shutter closed on my camera, Tom suggested we make our way hastily to the pub because a storm was approaching. Pedaling with intent through the outskirts of the New Forest, we made it onto the pavement just as it began to hail. A crescendo of pellets rained upon our crew just as we made it to the Trusty Servant pub’s shed overhang. Crowding under the eaves, we waited for a break in the hailstorm to sprint into the pub for drinks and a warm meal.

After a tasty pie and a few pints (sparkling water and lime for me), we all pedaled back to town and swung by the shop to continue to prep for Brother in the Wild. Under the azure cast of blue hour bliss, the windows of The Woods Cyclery lit up the now quiet street with a gilded glow.

I gathered my camera gear and walked down the street to my hotel, where I prepped to ride the wave of stoke into the Brother in the Wild weekend.

Brother in the Wild and Rat Run

The morning of our ride to Brother in the Wild, it was like navigating a temple of stacked plastic bins and bicycles. Everyone hurried about as they packed up a truck with goods to be transported out. Hannah had her notes, and Tom was making sure everything was okay.

The day prior, he and Robbie had driven out to the Brother in the Wild staging area to set up the tent when his car’s transmission blew, further complicating the day. Through all the excitement and bustle, Tom, Robbie, and Hannah were controlled and calm. Much to my surprise, we rolled out at 10 AM promptly, just as planned.

Tom worked with Will and James from Brother Cycles on the route options for Brother in the Wild. Some of the routing comes from The Woods’ own route called the Rat Run, a 160-mile mixed-terrain, multi-day ride that samples some of the best of the New Forest and Isle of Purbeck. Tom worked alongside Cass Gilbert on the routing and teased that more was on the way from the two.

Found On the Edge of the New Forest

Tom credits group rides for much of the shop’s success. When business is good, he and the team make the most of their free time spent riding – and it’s those bigger rides that truly strengthen their bonds. As Owen noted, the sacrifices that come with working at an independent shop feel worth it because of the community they’ve built around it. That sense of belonging, after all, is one of the foundations of a happy, fulfilled life.

Being on the edge of the New Forest only deepens that connection. It offers endless space to unwind, reconnect with nature, and lose yourself in its trails.

Hanging out with Tom throughout the week and into the weekend at Brother in the Wild, you can still see the little kid in him. He has a sparkle in his eye when he talks about riding. He’s the same kid who just wants to get lost in the woods and sleep under the stars. But this time, he’s got a whole team of dedicated, impassioned cyclists with him.

To witness what they’ve forged together is something as unique as it is beautiful.

Incubator for Community

Bike shops can be incubators for community when people are empowered. Empowerment nurtures passion, and passion is infectious. Customers and visitors feel it. People like me feel it. Hell, even Cari felt it, saying, “I would go to The Woods and just hang out. The people, the food, and the coffee made for a welcoming experience.”

It’s all so electric and infectious, and unlike many things on social media, The Woods actually lives up to the image it presents.

The Woods Cyclery and what it represents are much bigger than its tiny storefront. It’s a new paradigm for bike shops to succeed, and if you’re ever in the UK, it’s well worth the trek out to the edge of the New Forest to see Tom and the gang.

Thanks to all of you. You made me feel like family. I can’t recall a more emotional departure. Cari and I really felt the love.

Check out The Woods Cyclery on Instagram.

The Woods Cyclery

56 High St,
Lyndhurst, SO43 7BG,
United Kingdom

Monday – Saturday 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM
Sundays 9:00 AM to 4:00 PM

The post Inside / Out at The Woods Cyclery in the New Forest appeared first on The Radavist.
author: John Watson
